thread.topic: Retention is a content problem, not a check-ins problem

tweets.body:
Your content isn't just for new clients.
Your current ones are watching.

Every post you put out, your clients see it too.
They're not judging your hook.
They're asking one question: "Is this still the coach I want to be associated with?"

Retention isn't a results problem.
It's an identity problem.
And your content is where that identity gets reinforced or quietly killed.

The moment your client sees your post and thinks "that's my coach, hell yeah" — retention just happened.
The moment they scroll past hoping their mates don't see it — they're already gone.

image.prompt: Gritty editorial illustration of a muscular man looking at his phone on a gym floor at night, his expression intense and approving, lit by a single overhead gym light. Matte black and deep shadow palette with a single bold yellow accent on the phone screen glow. Moody, cinematic, slightly raw, not polished. Landscape orientation. No text, no logos, no faces that resemble real people.
image.alt: Gym floor scene, client reacting to a coach's post on their phone

tweets.body:
Weak content doesn't just cost you leads.
It costs you the clients you already have.
Every generic post is a small withdrawal from their belief in you.

Most coaches post to attract.
The best ones post to reinforce.
Same content does both, but only if it makes your current clients sit up straighter, not cringe in the group chat.

Next post you write, ask one question:
Will my current clients screenshot this and send it to a mate, or hope nobody they know sees it?
That's your retention strategy.
